84f91b4b-f9f5-55d9-9779-2d5488e2576a.date_of_birth_raw: born at Lebanon, Conn., in the year 1807
84f91b4b-f9f5-55d9-9779-2d5488e2576a.obit_text:
Rev. Roswell Park, D. D., Rector of Emmanuel Hall, class of 1831, at Lake View, near Chicago, Ill., July 16, 1869, aged 61 years.

The Church Register (Protestant Episcopal), for August, 1869, contains an obituary of Dr. Park which is preserved in the Records of the Association, and from which the following extracts are made:

"We cannot give this record of the departure of one who for seventeen years had been prominently connected with parochial and educational labor in the Northwest, without some fuller particulars of a life and work which so many remember with love and gratitude. He was born at Lebanon, Conn., in the year 1807, and at the early age of sixteen was employed by the United States authorities in making plans, &c., for the armory at Springfield. Shortly after this, his parents having already removed to the State of New York, he studied during the summers at Oxford and Hamilton Academies, while in the winters he taught school. Having prepared himself in this way by mingled study and teaching, he entered the Sophomore class at Hamilton College in the year 1826. In the following year he received an appointment as a Cadet of West Point, and after passing through the course there, graduated with great honor as head of his class. It was in accordance with the persevering industry which was ever a mark of his character, that within a month of his graduation at the Military Academy he passed the necessary examinations and received the degree of A. B. at Union College. Being now a lieutenant in the Engineer Corps of the Army, in the year 1833 he assisted in the construction of Fort Warren, and three years later he took charge of the construction of the Delaware Breakwater. The same year he was elected Professor of Philosophy and Chemistry in the University of Pennsylvania, and resigned his position in the Army. On the 10th of September, 1843, he was admitted to the Diaconate. In the following year he was advanced to the Priesthood. About this time he removed to Pomfret, Conn., where, until his removal to the West, he conducted a select school. It was in the year 1852 that he determined to remove to Racine, and there began what he felt was greatly needed in that portion of the Northwest, a Church College. For seven years he continued to labor most earnestly as President of this institution, and in 1859 he resigned the Presidency only to accept the more dignified office of Chancellor, and to fill the place of Professor of Natural Science. To these duties he added the laborious care of the large and growing parish of St. Luke in the city of Racine.

For the last six years of his life he was engaged in building up Emmanuel Hall, at Lake View, near Chicago. Quietly and earnestly, and with full measure of success, he devoted himself to his work and met with his reward. There was something in accordance with the steady, well-ordered dutifulness and exactness which were especially a mark of his character, that he should have been permitted to finish his school year, complete his examinations, and send his scholars to their homes with his parting benediction, before the summons came which was to call him unto God.

The spot chosen for his resting place is a beautiful and appropriate one. Those who have visited Racine College will, no doubt, remember the quiet place at the east end of the chapel. In sight of the lake, beneath the shadow of the trees, with the altar of God so near at hand, it was meet to lay one down to rest whose life had shown forth so happy a blending of educational and priestly labor."
84f91b4b-f9f5-55d9-9779-2d5488e2576a.interment_location: east end of the chapel, Racine College, Racine

f845ce8e-b6db-543a-99b9-9e5b2751e194.obit_text:
Lieut.-Colonel Andrew W. Bowman, U. S. Infantry, unassigned, class of 1841, at Omaha, Neb., July 17, 1869.

The particulars of Colonel Bowman's death have not been furnished the Association, but his etat de service is preserved in Cullum's Biographical Register of Graduates, and shows that after graduating, he was successively promoted to be Brevet 2d Lieut. of the 1st Infantry; 2d Lieut. 3d Infantry; 1st Lieut. 3d Infantry; Capt. 3d Infantry; Major 9th Infantry; and to the grade which he held at the time of his death. He served throughout the Mexican war, and was breveted Captain, U. S. Army, for gallant and meritorious conduct in the battle of Cerro Gordo.

bd6b1bd6-df4b-5186-9681-9226f30ec92b.confidence_notes: Entry states only: "Information of Mr. Cooper's death was supplied at so late a moment that no sketch of his life could be prepared."

8723952f-a253-5991-ad66-b3253420ba34.obit_text:
Rev. George Augustine Sterling, class of 1829, at Sharon, Conn., Oct. 17, 1869. The following notice of Mr. Sterling's life was furnished by the Hon. John Cotton Smith, of Sharon, Conn., through the kindness of General Cady, of the class of 1829.

Mr. Sterling graduated at an early age, at the U. S. Military Academy, and afterward served for a time in the U. S. Army, being stationed at Fort Gibson, in what was then the territory of Arkansas. His health failing, he resigned his commission, and, after the usual study of divinity, was ordained a clergyman of the Protestant Episcopal Church. He was settled for some years over the church at Huntington, Conn., and upon the giving up of this charge returned to his home in Sharon, and passed the remainder of his life at the place of his birth and early associations.

Mr. Sterling possessed a kindly disposition, a gentlemanly deportment, and a sincere and excellent piety. His favorite topic of conversation was religion, and his charity was great for all who bore the Christian name. He was an obliging neighbor and a good citizen, and in all the relations of life was esteemed, respected, and beloved.

"The sweet remembrance of the just
Will flourish when they sleep in dust."
8723952f-a253-5991-ad66-b3253420ba34.author: Hon. John Cotton Smith, of Sharon, Conn.
8723952f-a253-5991-ad66-b3253420ba34.confidence_notes: Surname printed "STERLTNG" (OCR); corrected to Sterling per repeated later occurrences.

f9454208-5f1f-5e34-b060-b6ad7c5e5cdb.location_of_death: on board the schooner Narragansett, in Charleston Harbor, S. C.
f9454208-5f1f-5e34-b060-b6ad7c5e5cdb.obit_text:
Brevet-Major George Burroughs, Captain, Corps of Engineers, class of 1862, on board the schooner Narragansett, in Charleston Harbor, S. C., Jan. 22, 1870.

From private sources and a published obituary, the following particulars in regard to Major Burroughs are gathered:

He was born in Camden, N. J., Dec. 11, 1841, and was the son of Rev. Henry Burroughs, Jr., then rector of St. Paul's Church, Camden, now of Christ Church, Boston, Mass., and of Sarah Tilden Burroughs. He removed with his parents to Northampton, Mass., in 1843, and thence to Boston in 1852. He was a member of the Boston Latin School for five years, and graduated third in his class in 1857, and obtained a Franklin Medal. He entered Harvard University without any "conditions" in the same year, and at once assumed the first rank in scholarship in his class. He left college to enter the Military Academy in 1858. In 1862 he was graduated third in his class, and was promoted to be 2d Lieut. Corps of Engineers, and from this grade subsequently to be 1st Lieut. and Captain. For gallant and meritorious conduct at the battle of Chickamauga, he was breveted Captain, and for faithful and meritorious services during the rebellion, to be Major, U. S. Army. He was married Oct. 18, 1866, in Cincinnati, Ohio, to Miss Carrie Bryson, of that city.

He left Charleston, S. C., Jan. 14, 1870, on a tour of inspection in the Narragansett, arrived at Cape Romain on the 17th, completed the inspection and repairs of the light-house there, and left on the 19th. On that evening he was taken ill with congestion of the brain, and died at 8.20 P. M., after his arrival in Charleston Harbor. His remains were taken to Boston and interred in Forest Hill Cemetery.

It is rare, indeed, said his former commanding officer, that we are called to mourn for one of such high endowments, joined with so many personal kindly traits; whose candid, frank, and joyous nature, and most marked independence of thought and character were such as to endear him to all his official associates of every grade, as well as to the many personal friends of this home of his boyhood.

1681f076-e09a-5822-8dd4-5d8537ac3e18.obit_text:
Major William H. Chase, formerly of the U. S. Corps of Engineers, class of 1815, at Jacksonville, Fla., Feb. 1, 1870.

The etat de service of Major Chase, from date of appointment until Oct. 31, 1856, is contained in Cullum's Biographical Register of Graduates, No. 150. From 1856 to 1861 he was President of the Alabama & Florida R. R. Co. No portion of his history from 1861 until the date of his decease has been furnished the Association.

de461d92-68e5-532f-9628-2c9baf61ad88.obit_text:
Lieut.-Colonel Thompson Morris, U. S. Army, class of 1822, retired, at Cincinnati, Ohio, Feb. 13, 1870.

Information of Col. Morris' death has but just been furnished, and the particulars are not therefore known. His official record includes continuous promotion from Cadet to be Second Lieutenant, First Lieutenant, and Captain of the 2d U. S. Infantry; to be Major of the 1st, and Lieut. Colonel of the 4th Infantry, and to be Brevet Major U. S. Army for gallant and meritorious conduct at the battle of Cerro Gordo, and Brevet Lieut.-Colonel U. S. Army, for gallant and meritorious conduct at the battles of Contreras and Cherubusco.

bed2b436-1109-5ea5-9d1a-e56363c8d0ca.obit_text:
2d Lieut. A. Tracy Lee, 5th U. S. Art., class of 1867, at Sedgwick Barracks, Washington, D. C., Feb. 19, 1870.

From official and private sources the following facts relating to Lieut. Lee are gathered:

He was born in Roxbury, Mass., Sept. 29, 1844, and was the eldest son of William Raymond, and Helen Maria Amory Lee. Upon graduating at the Military Academy, he was commissioned Second Lieutenant 5th U. S. Artillery, assigned to Co. "K," and stationed at the Dry Tortugas. Thence in January, 1868, he went with his company to New Orleans, and in April following was detailed for temporary duty with Co. "I," 4th U. S. Cavalry. In June, 1868, he joined Light Battery "F" of his regiment at Richmond, Va., and while serving with this Battery at Washington died of diphtheria. His decease was most sudden and unexpected, and cast a deep gloom over the extended circle of his friends. His short life presented little for record save an honorable observance of all duties. He was a pure-minded gentleman in his conduct, affectionate in his disposition, and considerate in his intercourse with others, and he was sincerely beloved by his comrades. Nothing more could well be said of so young a man and officer, who had no opportunities of proving his qualities beyond the common routine of duty. He was buried Feb. 22, 1870, at Forest Hill Cemetery, near Boston, Mass.
bed2b436-1109-5ea5-9d1a-e56363c8d0ca.interment_location: Forest Hill Cemetery, near Boston, Mass.
bed2b436-1109-5ea5-9d1a-e56363c8d0ca.confidence_notes: Middle name printed as "TRAcT" (OCR); read as "Tracy". First name given only as initial "A."

7314c87b-d621-53f9-a2d9-bb388430d567.obit_text:
Capt. Kenelm Robbins, U. S. Infantry, unassigned, class of 1863, at Jackson, Miss., Feb. 28, 1870.

Captain Robbins, from the time of his graduation, was successively promoted to be 2d Lieut., and 1st Lieut. 5th U. S. Cavalry, Captain 43d U. S. Infantry, Brevet 1st Lieut. U. S. Army, for gallant and meritorious services at the battle of Brandy Station, Va., and Brevet Captain U. S. A. for gallant and meritorious services at the battle of Winchester, Va. Further details of his life have not been furnished the Association.

bf945a18-db31-5ea1-bbb8-7834f3e40bde.obit_text:
Brevet Brig.-Gen. Morris S. Miller, Lieut.-Col. Dep. Q. M. Gen. U. S. Army, class of 1834, at New Orleans, La., March 11, 1870.

General Miller's official record, which is a long and honorable one, extending from his appointment as a Cadet through the successive grades of 2d Lieut., 1st Lieut. 3d U. S. Art.; Captain and Q. M., Captain 3d Art.; Major and Q. M. and Lieut.-Colonel, and Dep. Q. M. General, with Brevets of 2d Lieut. 3d Art.; Lieut.-Colonel, Colonel and Brig. General U. S. Army, is found in Cullum's Register, No. 763. From a published obituary the following extract is made:

"The funeral services were held in Trinity Church—the Church of his childhood and of his adoption in mature years—which was built and maintained chiefly by his father, Judge Morris S. Miller. A part of Gen. Miller's education was obtained at the school known as the High School, kept for many years by Mr. Charles Bartlett. In 1830 he went to the Military Academy, and from the time he graduated his life was passed in the discharge of his duties as a soldier of the U. S. Army, most of the time absent from his birth-place, but always hoping to make it his final home. His life was characterized by integrity, a keen sense of honor, the simplicity of a child, and an unbounded hospitality.

"During the last war, when corruption and love of money preyed upon patriotism, Gen. Miller proved incorruptible, and with millions passing through his hands as Quartermaster, he never dreamed of using his position and opportunities for his own advantage. Few officers have been better known in the army than he, and none more generally beloved."

fd4a51b0-026f-5929-b19a-aa911d87919f.obit_text:
George Russel Sullivan Bowdoin, Esquire, Attorney and Counsellor-at-Law (was named George R. Sullivan, when he was graduated), class of 1829, at Maurigy's Hotel, London, Eng., March 14, 1870.

The following particulars are collated from the feeling obituaries which appeared in the leading journals of New York city:

He was born the 14th of Nov., 1809, and was, consequently, sixty years old on his last birthday. In 1832, he married a grand-daughter of Alexander Hamilton and retired from the army to study law. In no profession could he have failed to be distinguished, for to quick intelligence and cool judgment he united untiring industry. As the senior member of the firm of Bowdoin, Larocque & Barlow, he was intrusted, probably, with as many important negotiations, as many delicate secrets, and the management of as many trusts as any other lawyer in the city. How wise and safe an adviser, how careful, judicious, and upright a trustee he was, none can know so well as the many who have been benefited by his wisdom and integrity. But Mr. Bowdoin was not merely a man of honor and a sound lawyer—he was a gentleman. His manners—those minor morals—were exquisite. Strictly scrupulous of the self-respect of others, he was careful that others should be, or should be made to be, equally scrupulous of his own.

Upon coming of age, he assumed the name of Bowdoin, in compliance with the will of his maternal great uncle, James Bowdoin, the only son of Gov. Bowdoin, of Massachusetts. Although descended from a very high lineage, including among its members the Temples, the Bowdoins, the Winthrops, and the Sullivans, and allied to the Hamiltons, the Schuylers, and the Van Rensselaers, he was thoroughly democratic in his feelings and his deportment, and had he ever aspired to political distinction his personal popularity would have insured him success. But he confined himself exclusively to his profession, as a safer and more honorable career than any thing that the devious ways of modern politics could offer.

His remains are interred in the Cemetery at Tarrytown, N. Y.
fd4a51b0-026f-5929-b19a-aa911d87919f.confidence_notes: Surname printed "BOwDCIN" (OCR); corrected to Bowdoin per firm name and later occurrences. Graduated under the name George R. Sullivan.

bac2d967-ceaf-5502-91f5-cfb006794034.obit_text:
Brevet Maj.-Gen. James Wolfe Ripley, U. S. Army, class of 1814, at Hartford, Conn., March 15, 1870.

In general orders from the headquarters of the army, General Sherman, after announcing the death of General Ripley, recites his extended and useful career as an officer of the army, including his promotion from a Second Lieutenant of Artillery, through successive grades, to be Chief of Ordnance, and including also service in four wars; and continues—

"Another of our venerable officers has thus been called, leaving a character for purity, integrity, and excellence, through a long and useful life, to be emulated by his surviving comrades."

The published obituaries which followed his death were abundant in sincere tributes to the exalted elements of his character, and a particular instance especially serves to show the estimate in which he was held by others in the single-heartedness of purpose which actuated himself. "In 1854," says the Hartford Courant, "a committee of citizens, with George Ashmun at their head, tendered him a public dinner, in a letter paying the highest tribute to his 'unflinching integrity, energy, and fidelity.'

"He gratefully acknowledged the compliment, but declined the dinner, saying—

"'Public speaking is no part of my profession, and my taste and judgment have ever been opposed to any such demonstration as the reward for the simple performance of a soldier's duty.'" "And so," adds the journal, "he went through life; his utmost efforts were to him simply a soldier's duty."

General Ripley was born in Windham Co., Conn., Dec. 10, 1794, the son of Ralph and Eunice Huntington Ripley. He married Aug. 11, 1824, Sarah Denny, of Leicester, Mass., who still survives him. Of nine children, three daughters survive, two of them at their home in Hartford, and one in Boston, the wife of Mr. Robert C. Hooper.

The General was buried in the Cemetery at Springfield, on the 18th of March.
bac2d967-ceaf-5502-91f5-cfb006794034.confidence_notes: Birth year printed "1'94" (OCR); read as 1794, consistent with class of 1814.

24da2e64-9fd4-5cab-a8f2-362c4e0a1c69.obit_text:
Major-General George H. Thomas, U. S. Army, class of 1840, at San Francisco, Cal., March 28, 1870.

The details of this most irreparable loss are so fresh in the minds of all of us that they do not need repetition now. It is sufficient to say that the death of General Thomas called forth from every part of the country such sincere and unqualified expressions of sorrow as have rarely, if ever, before found utterance. His remains, transported across the Continent, were everywhere received by tearful mourners. They were followed to their present resting place in the Cemetery at Troy, N. Y., by the President of the United States, the Secretary of War, the General of the Army, with many other dignitaries of the general government; by Committees from the two Houses of Congress, the Governor and Legislature of the State of New York, and deputations from other States; by members of the Army of the Cumberland, who had learned to look up to him with filial reverence and devotion; and finally, by a vast concourse of people whose demeanor betokened a sense of personal affliction in the death of one who has been appropriately described as the perfected type of the American soldier. His record is here omitted because it is understood that his complete biography is in course of preparation for publication.

ec22cc12-3b68-554f-84a7-99436c1a26df.obit_text:
Captain Henry C. Wharton, formerly of the U. S. Corps of Engineers, class of 1862, at Baltimore, Md., April 8, 1870.

Captain Wharton was promoted from Cadet to be 2d Lieutenant of Engineers, and afterward successively to be 1st Lieutenant and Captain of Engineers, and to be Brevet Captain U. S. Army. He likewise held at one time the commission of Lieutenant-Colonel, U. S. Veteran Volunteer Engineers. He resigned his commission in the Army January 6, 1870, and his brief record in civil life has not been furnished the Association.
ec22cc12-3b68-554f-84a7-99436c1a26df.confidence_notes: First name printed "HEMRY" (OCR); corrected to Henry.

212a363c-b314-5855-a2d3-b38e949adb5a.obit_text:
Dr. Alfred Brush, class of 1832, at Detroit, Mich., April 12, 1870.

The following extract is from a published obituary of Dr. Brush:

"The sudden death of Dr. Alfred Brush, of this city, brother of Hon. Edmund A. Brush, took our citizens generally by surprise. The immediate cause of his death was congestion of the lungs, although his general health had been any thing but good for many years. Dr. Brush was an old resident, although scarcely passed the meridian of life, for he was born in 1814. He was educated at West Point, and although he was prostrated by a severe and dangerous illness while there, which interrupted his studies for several months, he graduated high up among the number assigned to the Artillery. From the effect of the illness at West Point he never recovered. Soon after he graduated he was put in charge of the government works at Monroe, Mich., where he was engaged when the Florida war broke out. He felt that his health was not equal to the discharge of his duty in the field, yet he would not ask to be relieved, but promptly joined his regiment, where he served the country faithfully and with great credit. But this strain upon his constitution was too great, and he returned home prostrated by severe sickness, which finally rendered him unfit for duty to that extent that he was compelled to tender his resignation. After he left the Army he pursued the study of medicine under the celebrated Dr. Mott of New York city, and graduated as a physician and surgeon with high honors. His mind was peculiarly adapted to the investigation of subjects which required patience, great care, diligence, and industry. While he was very reticent to those with whom he was not well acquainted, he was always pleasant, kind, and very courteous to his friends, and ready to serve them to the last extent.

"The loss of such a man, although it makes no void among public men, is one which can never be forgotten by those who had the good fortune to be among his intimate friends and associates. To them it is irreparable."

ee9fcc66-d820-58da-9787-47721c2361fe.obit_text:
Mr. George W. Rose, class of 1852, at Detroit, Mich., May 19, 1870. The following is an extract from an obituary of Mr. Rose:

"Mr. Geo. W. Rose, recently of the firm of H. P. Baldwin & Co., died at the residence of his father-in-law, Governor Baldwin, yesterday at 12.30 P. M. Mr. Rose was a son of Hon. Robert L. Rose, of Allen's Hill, near Canandaigua, N. Y., where he was born in February, 1832. He was appointed a Cadet at the United States Military Academy in his sixteenth year, and graduated with distinction, being fourth in his class. After serving in the Army as a Lieutenant of Topographical Engineers, on the Lake Survey at Detroit, and as an Assistant Professor at the Military Academy, he married, in 1856, a daughter of Hon. H. P. Baldwin, of this city, and in the fall of the same year resigned his commission in the Army, his health being then impaired. He engaged at once in business, and continued so engaged until 1867, when, on account of his ill-health, he was obliged to give up all business pursuits, and, together with his wife, he spent the winter of 1867-8 in Texas, the winter of 1868-9 in Aiken, S. C., and the past winter in Florida. During the last winter he failed very rapidly, suffering from consumption. Some four weeks ago his father and sister visited him and returned with him very much enfeebled to New York. Last Thursday he arrived again in this city, accompanied by his father, sister, and brother-in-law, the latter a physician who has remained constantly with him since Mr. Rose's arrival in New York.

Mr. Rose, being a graduate of West Point, was on the point a number of times during the late war of offering his services to the country, but was prevailed upon by his friends and relations, in consequence of his poor health and partial deafness, to desist from such an undertaking. He leaves a family of a wife and two children."
